Between November 1966 and December 1967, over a hundred witnesses reported encounters with the creature — which became known as the 'Mothman' — alongside anomalous aerial lights, Men in Black visits, and prophetic communications attributed to a figure called Indrid Cold. The 13-month wave climaxed on December 15, 1967, with the catastrophic collapse of the Silver Bridge at Point Pleasant, killing 46 people, an event many witnesses associated with the prior anomalous activity. Researcher John Keel documented the full case in The Mothman Prophecies (1975), proposing connections between Mothman, UAP activity, and ultraterrestrial intelligence. The Point Pleasant events remain among the most extensively documented multi-witness cryptid and anomalous phenomena cases in the United States. Classification: Unverified encounter claim.
sources: Keel, J., The Mothman Prophecies (1975)
Sergent, D. and Wamsley, J., Mothman: Behind the Red Eyes (2002)
Point Pleasant Register, November 16, 1966 — first press account

title: Malmstrom AFB Missile Shutdown Incident
description: On March 16, 1967, ten Minuteman I ICBM launch facilities (LF) at Echo Flight, Malmstrom Air Force Base, Montana, went offline in rapid succession. Launch officer Captain Robert Salas reports that security personnel had alerted him to a glowing red object hovering above the launch control facility at the time of the shutdowns. Similar shutdowns were reported at Oscar Flight later the same month. Declassified U.S. Air Force documents confirm the malfunctions; the cause was officially attributed to a 'spurious signal' in the logic coupler. Robert Hastings's subsequent research located multiple witnesses. Classification: CE-2 (UAP sighting with coincident equipment effects).
sources: Hastings, R., UFOs and Nukes: Extraordinary Encounters at Nuclear Weapons Sites (2008)
Salas, R., declassified USAF testimony
USAF Missile Incident Report, Malmstrom AFB (1967, declassified)

title: Falcon Lake Incident — Stefan Michalak CE-2 with Radiation Injuries, Manitoba
description: On May 20, 1967, amateur geologist Stefan Michalak was prospecting near Falcon Lake, Manitoba, Canada, when two oval-shaped objects descended from the sky; one landed approximately 160 feet from his position on a flat rock surface. Michalak approached and observed the landed object — which he estimated at 35–40 feet in diameter with a highly polished surface — for approximately 30 minutes, noting a sulphurous odour and hearing an internal whirring sound. When a panel on the object opened and a hot gas or directed exhaust struck his chest, his shirt and undershirt were set alight and he fell backward; when he reached for the object, his glove was scorched. He became severely nauseated and vomited repeatedly before making his way out of the forest. A physician who examined him that evening noted a distinctive grid-patterned burn across his chest and abdomen. Over subsequent weeks and months, Michalak suffered persistent symptoms medically consistent with radiation exposure: severe nausea and vomiting, dramatic weight loss (approximately 22 pounds in seven weeks), swollen lymph nodes, recurring episodes of fainting, and chronic fatigue. He was hospitalised multiple times; specialists at the Mayo Clinic in Rochester, Minnesota, evaluated him in 1968. The RCMP, the Canadian Department of National Defence, the Department of Health and Welfare, and officials from the Whiteshell Nuclear Research Establishment each conducted independent investigations of the landing site, measuring elevated radiation levels at the rock formation and finding a circle of altered soil. No conventional explanation — aircraft, hoax, or industrial exposure — was established as consistent with both the physical evidence and the medical record. The Falcon Lake case remains the most thoroughly government-investigated and medically documented CE-2 encounter involving verifiable radiation-consistent injuries in the North American UAP research literature. Classification: CE-2.
sources: Michalak, S., My Encounter with the UFO (Osnova Publications, 1967)
RCMP and Canadian government investigation files (1967–1969, partially declassified)
Rutkowski, C. and Demeara, G., The Canadian UFO Report (2006)

title: Patterson-Gimlin Film — Bigfoot Filmed at Bluff Creek, California
description: On October 20, 1967, former rodeo rider Roger Patterson and tracker Bob Gimlin filmed approximately 59 seconds of 16mm footage of a large, bipedal, hair-covered figure walking along a sandbar at Bluff Creek in northern California. The footage — now universally called the Patterson-Gimlin film — captured the subject (commonly designated 'Patty') from multiple angles as it walked away from the camera and turned to look directly at the lens. Patterson died in 1972; Gimlin maintained the authenticity of the encounter consistently for the following decades. Multiple forensic analyses have been conducted: physical anthropologists Grover Krantz and Jeff Meldrum argued that the subject's gait, limb proportions, and visible musculature were inconsistent with a human in a costume; biomechanical analyses published by Dmitri Bayanov and Igor Bourtsev similarly concluded the subject displayed non-human locomotive characteristics. Critics including costume designer Philip Morris, who claimed to have made the suit, consistently failed to produce a convincing reproduction under controlled conditions. Photogrammetric analysis places the subject's height at approximately six feet five inches with an estimated mass substantially exceeding that of a human adult male. The Patterson-Gimlin film remains the single most-analysed piece of physical evidence in Bigfoot research and has not been definitively demonstrated to be a fabrication. Classification: Physical evidence (authenticity contested).
sources: Meldrum, J., Sasquatch: Legend Meets Science (2006)
Krantz, G.S., Big Footprints: A Scientific Inquiry into the Reality of Sasquatch (1992)
Bayanov, D. and Bourtsev, I., On Neanderthal vs. Paranthropus (Current Anthropology, 1976)

title: Buff Ledge Abduction — Lake Champlain, Vermont
description: On August 7, 1968, two summer camp workers — 16-year-old Michael Lapp and 19-year-old Janet Cornell — at Camp Buff Ledge on Lake Champlain, Vermont, reported watching a luminous cigar-shaped aerial object release three smaller disc-shaped craft that performed dramatic manoeuvres over the lake at dusk. One disc descended and hovered directly over the dock where both were sitting; both subsequently experienced approximately two hours of unaccounted-for time and awoke in different positions with no memory of what had intervened. They did not speak about the experience with each other and went their separate ways after the summer season. A decade later, in 1979, investigator Walter Webb of the Charles Hayden Planetarium in Boston located both individuals and conducted independent hypnotic regression sessions without allowing prior communication between them. Both independently recalled — in detailed, structurally consistent accounts — being taken aboard the hovering craft, examined by small humanoid beings, and returned to the dock. Specific details of craft interior configuration, entity appearance, and procedural sequence showed a degree of mutual corroboration that Webb and NICAP investigators considered unlikely to arise from separate fabrication. Webb published his full investigation in Encounter at Buff Ledge: A UFO Case History (1994). The case is considered methodologically significant within abduction research because the decade of non-communication between witnesses substantially mitigates the contamination concerns that affect cases investigated immediately after disclosure. Classification: CE-3.
sources: Webb, W., Encounter at Buff Ledge: A UFO Case History (CUFOS, 1994)
NICAP case investigation file, 1979
Clark, J., The UFO Encyclopedia (1998)

title: Minot AFB UFO — B-52 Crew and Ground Radar Incident
description: On the night of October 24, 1968, an unidentified aerial object was tracked on radar and observed visually by multiple personnel at Minot Air Force Base, North Dakota, over a period of approximately three hours in events involving both airborne and ground-based observers. A B-52 crew returning to base reported a bright light closing rapidly on their aircraft and then flying formation alongside them for several minutes; the object appeared on the B-52's own radar at a range of approximately three miles and was simultaneously tracked on ground-based radar at Minot. A separate team of Air Force security policemen reported observing a glowing, reddish-orange object near one of the base's Minuteman ICBM launch facilities at low altitude. Project Blue Book received the incident report and classified it. Thomas Tulien and the Sign Historical Group published in 2005 the most comprehensive reconstruction of the incident, drawing on declassified documents, radar tapes, and testimony from 18 witnesses including the B-52 crew. The Minot case is considered one of the most complex and best-documented multi-witness nuclear facility UAP incidents of the Cold War era. Classification: CE-2.
sources: Tulien, T. and Hall, J., Sign Historical Group, Minot AFB UFO Incident Report (2005)
Project Blue Book files, Minot AFB case (October 1968)
Hastings, R., UFOs and Nukes: Extraordinary Encounters at Nuclear Weapons Sites (2008)

title: Vallée Proposes Control System and Interdimensional Hypothesis
description: Through a sequence of research books published between 1969 and 1988, French-American computer scientist and UAP researcher Jacques Vallée progressively developed what became the most sophisticated alternative to the extraterrestrial hypothesis: the control system and interdimensional model of UAP. In Passport to Magonia (1969), Vallée demonstrated structural parallels between modern UAP encounter reports and medieval European accounts of fairy contacts, elementals, and angels, suggesting the phenomenon was far older and more culturally embedded than the post-1947 framing acknowledged. In The Invisible College (1975), he proposed that the phenomenon functioned as a control system for human belief and behaviour, deliberately adapting its presentation to the cultural context of the witnesses. In Messengers of Deception (1979), he argued that the contactee movement showed signs of deliberate manipulation consistent with social engineering. In Dimensions: A Casebook of Alien Contact (1988), he explicitly articulated the interdimensional hypothesis — that UAP represented intrusions from parallel dimensions or higher-dimensional realities rather than craft from other star systems — citing the physical impossibility of interstellar travel times, the trickster character of encounter phenomenology, and the manifest inconsistency between entity descriptions and any plausible biology evolved under stellar conditions. Vallée's framework anticipated later developments in quantum information theory and consciousness research. His model remains the most coherent theoretical framework for UAP that incorporates the full breadth of historical and phenomenological evidence. Classification: Not applicable (theoretical research).
sources: Vallée, J., Passport to Magonia (1969)
Vallée, J., Dimensions: A Casebook of Alien Contact (1988)
Vallée, J., The Invisible College (1975)

title: Project Blue Book Disbanded
description: Following the University of Colorado's Condon Report, which concluded that further UFO investigation was unlikely to yield scientific benefit, the U.S. Air Force officially disbands Project Blue Book on December 17, 1969. The programme's 12,618-case archive is eventually declassified and transferred to the National Archives. J. Allen Hynek publicly disputes the Condon Report's conclusions.
sources: USAF press release, December 17, 1969
Condon, E.U. (ed.), Scientific Study of Unidentified Flying Objects (1969)
Hynek, J.A., The UFO Experience (1972)

title: US and Soviet Psychotronics / Psi-Warfare Research — Cold War Anomalous Cognition Programmes
description: By the early 1970s, both the United States and Soviet Union were investing substantially in the investigation of anomalous human cognition capabilities as potential intelligence and military tools. In the Soviet Union, research under the umbrella term 'psychotronics' had been underway since the 1960s, with government-funded institutes studying telepathy, psychokinesis, and long-range human influence under researchers including Leonid Vasiliev and Yuri Gulyaev. The Soviet programme was significantly more public than its American counterpart, generating multiple scientific publications and attracting KGB and GRU funding. In the United States, CIA contract research at Stanford Research Institute beginning in 1972 (the programme that would become Stargate) was initiated partly in response to intelligence assessments suggesting Soviet exploitation of parapsychological phenomena at a significant scale. A 1972 DIA report, declassified decades later, expressed concern about the potential 'brain warfare' implications of Soviet psi research. The parallel development of institutionalised psi research in both superpowers during the early 1970s represented a historically unprecedented convergence of state security apparatus and anomalous cognition investigation, generating the most systematic experimental dataset in the history of parapsychology to that point. Researcher Jim Schnabel documented this history in Remote Viewers: The Secret History of America's Psychic Spies (1997). Classification: Not applicable (intelligence programme history).
sources: Schnabel, J., Remote Viewers: The Secret History of America's Psychic Spies (1997)
DIA report, 'Soviet and Czechoslovakian Parapsychology Research' (1975, declassified)
Targ, R. and Puthoff, H.E., Mind-Reach: Scientists Look at Psychic Ability (1977)

title: SRI Remote Viewing Programme Begins
description: Physicists Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff at Stanford Research Institute (SRI) begin government-contracted laboratory investigations of remote viewing and other anomalous cognition phenomena. Initial work involves subjects Ingo Swann and Pat Price. CIA funding is provided under contract with SRI.
sources: Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff, 'Information Transmission Under Conditions of Sensory Shielding,' Nature (1974)
CIA FOIA release, Stargate programme documents

title: Pascagoula Abduction — Hickson and Parker
description: On the evening of October 11, 1973, Charles Hickson and Calvin Parker were fishing on the Pascagoula River, Mississippi, when they reported being seized by three pale humanoid beings with claw-like hands and transported into a hovering craft where they underwent a brief examination before being returned. Police placed both men in a room unaware of being recorded; their continued mutual distress and whispered corroboration in private significantly reinforced their credibility. Dr. James Harder (APRO) and Dr. J. Allen Hynek both conducted formal interviews and independently assessed the witnesses as sincere. Parker revealed additional details in his memoir Pascagoula — The Closest Encounter (2018). The case remains the most extensively investigated abduction claim in the southern United States. Classification: CE-3.
sources: Hickson, C. and Mendez, W., UFO Contact at Pascagoula (1983)
Hynek, J.A. and Harder, J., investigative reports (1973)
Parker, C., Pascagoula — The Closest Encounter: My Story (2018)

title: Coyne Helicopter Encounter — Mansfield, Ohio
description: On the night of October 18, 1973, U.S. Army Reserve Captain Lawrence J. Coyne and his three-man helicopter crew were flying a UH-1H Huey from Columbus to Cleveland, Ohio, at 2,500 feet altitude when they observed a red light approaching from the east on an apparent collision course. As Coyne pushed the helicopter into a powered descent, the object — described as a large, grey, cigar-shaped craft approximately 60 feet long — slowed and stopped to hover overhead, emitting a green beam of light that filled the cockpit. The helicopter's magnetic compass oscillated and both UHF and VHF radios failed completely during proximity. Despite the helicopter being in a 20-degree nose-down power-off descent, the altitude indicator showed the aircraft climbing from 1,700 to 3,500 feet at approximately 1,000 feet per minute — an ascent that no crew member had commanded and for which no mechanical explanation was subsequently identified. A family of five driving below independently observed the encounter and reported it to authorities. CUFOS investigator Jennie Zeidman conducted an exhaustive multi-year investigation, interviewing all crew members and the independent ground witnesses separately. Hynek presented the case to the United Nations in 1978 as an example of a mechanically anomalous close encounter. Classification: CE-2.
sources: Zeidman, J., A Helicopter-UFO Encounter over Ohio, CUFOS (1979)
Coyne, L.J., Army Reserve witness statement (1973)
Hynek, J.A., presentation to United Nations Secretary-General's panel (1978)

title: Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS) Founded by J. Allen Hynek
description: In late 1973, Dr. J. Allen Hynek — astronomer, longtime Project Blue Book scientific consultant, and author of The UFO Experience (1972) — founded the Center for UFO Studies (CUFOS) in Evanston, Illinois, establishing the first independent scientific organisation devoted to systematic UAP investigation using professional research standards. CUFOS was explicitly created to fill the institutional vacuum left by Project Blue Book's 1969 closure and to apply the rigorous case investigation and data archival methods that Hynek had found lacking in the Air Force programme. The centre published the International UFO Reporter journal and the Journal of UFO Studies, and trained a network of field investigators. Hynek's CE (Close Encounter) classification system — distinguishing CE-1 (visual sightings), CE-2 (physical effects), CE-3 (entity observations), and later CE-4 (abduction) — was formalised through CUFOS and became the international standard for categorising UAP events. CUFOS also provided the scientific infrastructure for major case investigations including the Coyne helicopter encounter (1973), the Kaikoura films analysis, and extensive abduction case documentation. Following Hynek's death in 1986, CUFOS continued under director Mark Rodeghier. The organisation represents the foundational institution of the systematic independent UAP research tradition. Classification: Not applicable (research institution history).
sources: Hynek, J.A., The UFO Experience: A Scientific Inquiry (1972)
Center for UFO Studies archives and publications, Evanston, Illinois
Clark, J., The UFO Encyclopedia (1998)

title: Berwyn Mountains Incident — North Wales
description: On the evening of January 23, 1974, residents across multiple communities in the Berwyn Mountain range of North Wales reported a massive explosion or impact sound coinciding with a magnitude 3.5 earthquake, accompanied by sustained bright lights visible on the hillside above Llandrillo. Nurse Pat Evans drove to the scene and reported observing at close range a large glowing object resting on the hillside — an account she maintained consistently for decades. Military and police investigation attributed the event to the seismic activity combined with a simultaneous meteor sighting and poachers' lights. Civilian researchers including Margaret Fry and Nick Redfern disputed this composite explanation as insufficient to account for Evans's close observation and early military presence at the site. The incident remains debated between conventional and anomalous interpretations. Classification: CE-1 (disputed).
sources: Evans, P., witness testimony (1974 and subsequently)
Redfern, N., Strange Secrets (2003)
Fry, M., Who Are They? (2004)

title: Targ and Puthoff Remote Viewing Results Published in Nature
description: A paper by Russell Targ and Harold Puthoff reporting statistically significant results in remote viewing experiments with Ingo Swann and Pat Price is published in Nature, Vol. 251. The paper prompts sustained scientific debate. Results from 51 outdoor target trials showed above-chance correspondence by independent blind judges.
sources: Targ, R. and Puthoff, H.E., Nature 251, 602–607 (1974)

title: Moody Publishes Life After Life
description: Physician Raymond Moody publishes Life After Life, compiling accounts from over 150 individuals resuscitated from clinical death or near-death. The book introduces the term 'near-death experience' into scientific and popular discourse and identifies a recurrent phenomenological pattern including out-of-body perception, tunnel experience, and life review.
sources: Raymond Moody, Life After Life (1975)
International Association for Near-Death Studies

title: Stevenson Publishes Cases of the Reincarnation Type, Volume I
description: Psychiatrist Ian Stevenson of the University of Virginia publishes the first volume of Cases of the Reincarnation Type, presenting systematic field investigations of children reporting memories of previous lives. The four-volume series ultimately documents over 2,500 cases with detailed correspondence analysis.
sources: Ian Stevenson, Cases of the Reincarnation Type Vol. I: India (1975)
University of Virginia Division of Perceptual Studies

title: Travis Walton Abduction — Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest
description: On the evening of November 5, 1975, logger Travis Walton and six co-workers in the Apache-Sitgreaves National Forest near Snowflake, Arizona, reported encountering a large glowing disc-shaped craft hovering at treetop height. When Walton approached, a beam of blue-green light struck him and he was thrown backward; his crew fled the scene. Walton was missing for five days before reappearing disoriented on a roadside near Heber, Arizona, reporting an abduction experience involving humanoid entities. All six crew members passed polygraph examinations regarding their account; Walton also passed independent polygraph testing. The National Enquirer's UFO investigation panel named it the best UAP case of 1975. The incident was the basis for the film Fire in the Sky (1993). Classification: CE-3.
sources: Walton, T., The Walton Experience (1978; republished as Fire in the Sky, 1996)
APRO investigation report (1975)
Lorenzen, C. and J., APRO Bulletin (November 1975)

title: Tehran F-4 Intercept Encounter
description: Two Iranian Air Force F-4 Phantom fighters scrambled to intercept an unidentified aerial object over Tehran experience sequential avionics and weapons system failures at close range. A DIA report filed the following day characterises the case as meeting all criteria for a valid UAP study, citing radar-visual correlation, credible trained witnesses, electromagnetic effects, and sustained duration.
sources: DIA Report: Iran: Unidentified Flying Object, September 19, 1976 (declassified)
Kean, L., UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record (2010)

title: Dover Demon — Massachusetts Entity Sightings
description: On the night of April 21–22, 1977, three young witnesses in Dover, Massachusetts, independently reported encounters with a small, pale, large-headed entity on two consecutive nights. Bill Bartlett, then 17, first observed the creature on Farm Street at approximately 10:30 pm on April 21, describing it as roughly four feet tall with a disproportionately large, watermelon-shaped head, glowing orange eyes, and elongated spidery fingers; he sketched the figure immediately afterward. A second witness, John Baxter, independently encountered the creature on Miller Hill Road the same night. A third sighting was reported the following night by Abby Brabham and Will Taintor. All four witnesses were interviewed separately by cryptozoologist Loren Coleman, who named the entity the 'Dover Demon' and published the case in Mysterious America (1983). The creature bore a strong superficial resemblance to contemporary descriptions of Grey-type entities from CE3 encounter accounts, and the case has been analysed both within the cryptid and close-encounter research traditions. No physical evidence was recovered and no further sightings were reported. Classification: Unverified encounter claim.
sources: Coleman, L., Mysterious America (1983)
Coleman, L. and Clark, J., Cryptozoology A to Z (1999)
Dover local press, April 1977

title: Colares UFO Flap — Operation Saucer, Brazilian Amazon
description: Between August 1977 and March 1978, the island of Colares and surrounding communities in Pará state, northern Brazil, experienced an intense wave of encounters during which residents reported being struck by narrow beams of light from unidentified objects, suffering burns, puncture marks, anaemia-like symptoms, and documented physical injuries. The Brazilian Air Force conducted an official investigation designated Operação Prato (Operation Saucer), led by Captain Uyrangê Hollanda, accumulating over 500 photographs, 16mm film footage, and accounts from approximately 300 military and civilian witnesses. Hollanda gave a detailed interview to journalist A.J. Gevaerd in 1997, shortly before his death, stating that the phenomena were real and unexplained. Selected mission files were declassified and published by the Brazilian Air Force in 2004. The Colares case involves more independently documented physical injuries from UAP proximity than almost any other case in the research literature. Classification: CE-2.
sources: Operação Prato files, Brazilian Air Force (partially declassified 2004)
Hollanda, U., interview with Gevaerd, A.J., UFO Magazine Brazil (1997)
Pratt, B., UFO Danger Zone (1996)

title: Kaikoura Lights — New Zealand Film and Radar Correlation
description: On the night of December 30–31, 1978, a television film crew from Channel 0 (Melbourne) aboard an Argosy cargo aircraft filmed a series of anomalous luminous objects for approximately ten minutes along the Kaikoura coast of New Zealand's South Island. Air Traffic Control at Christchurch tracked unidentified radar returns in the region during the filming period, and the aircraft crew observed and described the objects visually in real time. The footage was broadcast internationally and prompted scientific attention. Independent analysis by Dr. Bruce Maccabee — a U.S. Navy optical physicist — and other researchers ruled out squid-boat lights, Venus, and aircraft for all sequences, concluding the footage recorded physically real, unidentified aerial objects. The case remains one of the few UAP events involving correlated civilian film, ATC radar, and airborne visual confirmation simultaneously. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Maccabee, B., photometric analysis, MUFON Symposium Proceedings (1979)
Fogarty, Q., Let's Hope They're Friendly (1982)
Klass, P.J., UFOs: The Public Deceived (1983) [skeptical perspective]

title: Princeton PEAR Laboratory Founded
description: Robert G. Jahn, Dean of Engineering at Princeton University, establishes the Princeton Engineering Anomalies Research (PEAR) laboratory to conduct systematic studies of possible mind-matter interaction using electronic random event generators. The laboratory operates until 2007 and accumulates approximately 2.5 million experimental trials.
sources: Robert G. Jahn and Brenda J. Dunne, Margins of Reality (1987)
PEAR Technical Note Series, Princeton University

title: Rendlesham Forest Incident
description: USAF security personnel at RAF Woodbridge, Suffolk, England, observe and approach an unidentified structured object in Rendlesham Forest. Over the following two nights, Deputy Base Commander Lieutenant Colonel Charles Halt leads a team that observes a pulsing object directing light beams toward the base. Physical trace evidence, elevated radiation readings, and the official Halt memorandum document the incidents.
sources: Halt, C., Memorandum to UK Ministry of Defence, January 13, 1981 (declassified 1983)
UK National Archives, UFO Files series (2008–2013)

title: Cash-Landrum Incident — Road Encounter and Radiation Injuries, Texas
description: On the night of December 29, 1980, Betty Cash, Vickie Landrum, and Vickie's seven-year-old grandson Colby Landrum encountered a large, diamond-shaped object emitting intense heat and bright flame from its underside while driving on Farm Road 1485 near Huffman, Texas. The object was escorted by approximately 23 twin-rotor CH-47 Chinook military helicopters, suggesting direct U.S. military involvement. The heat from the craft was intense enough to damage the vehicle's dashboard; all three witnesses experienced acute radiation-consistent symptoms in the days and weeks following, including hair loss, vomiting, skin blistering, and eye inflammation. Betty Cash was hospitalised with severe symptoms on multiple occasions and died in 1998; the Landrum family experienced ongoing health complications. UFO researcher John Schuessler, a NASA contractor, investigated the case and documented the medical evidence in detail. The Landrums filed a civil suit against the U.S. government in 1981 under the Federal Tort Claims Act; it was dismissed in 1986 on the grounds that no identified U.S. military vehicle matched the witnesses' description. The Cash-Landrum case remains one of the most medically documented CE2 events in the research literature, combining multi-witness testimony, physical vehicle effects, and clinical evidence of ionising radiation exposure. Classification: CE2.
sources: Schuessler, J., The Cash-Landrum UFO Incident (1998)
Schuessler, J., report to the Fund for UFO Research (1982)
Landrum v. United States, Civil Action No. H-84-1717, U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas (1986)

title: Trans-en-Provence Landing Trace Case — GEPAN Investigation
description: At approximately 5 p.m. on January 8, 1981, retired engineer Renato Nicolai reported observing a metallic saucer-shaped object approximately 2.5 metres in diameter land briefly on his property at Trans-en-Provence, Var, France, emitting a faint whistling sound before ascending rapidly. A circular trace approximately 2.2 metres in diameter was found at the landing site and sampled the following day by the Gendarmerie. GEPAN (the official French UAP study group under CNES) conducted a multi-laboratory investigation, finding: soil compressed by forces consistent with 4–5 tonnes; soil heated to 300–600°C; anomalous mineral deposits; and chlorophyll reduction of 30–50% in alfalfa plants proximate to the trace, with premature senescence correlated with proximity to the landing point. GEPAN Technical Note 16 concluded that a strong, unexplained physical event had occurred at the site. The case is the most thoroughly scientifically documented physical-trace UAP landing case in the published literature. Classification: CE-2.
sources: GEPAN Technical Note 16, CNES (1983)
Velasco, J.-J., Journal of Scientific Exploration 4(1) (1990)
Vallée, J., Confrontations (1990)

title: Sabom Publishes Recollections of Death — First Medical Investigation of NDEs
description: In 1982, Atlanta cardiologist Michael Sabom published Recollections of Death: A Medical Investigation, the first systematic medical study of near-death experiences conducted by a clinician with direct access to resuscitated cardiac patients. Sabom had initially approached Raymond Moody's Life After Life (1975) as a committed sceptic; his subsequent structured interviews with 116 resuscitated patients produced 67 NDE accounts, many with detailed out-of-body components. His most critical finding concerned a subset of patients who reported accurate, independently verifiable details of their own resuscitation procedures — details they claimed to have observed from an elevated external vantage point. When compared with a control group of cardiac patients without NDE reports, the NDE-reporting subgroup accurately described specific, non-intuitive procedural details — placement of electrodes, unusual defibrillation sequences, specific equipment configurations — that the control group consistently failed to match. Sabom's work provided the first systematic clinical evidence for the veridical component of the NDE and established the methodological precedent for subsequent prospective studies including those of Pim van Lommel (2001) and Sam Parnia (AWARE study, 2014). Classification: Scientific research publication.
sources: Sabom, M., Recollections of Death: A Medical Investigation (Harper & Row, 1982)
Sabom, M., Light and Death (Zondervan, 1998)
Greyson, B., The Near-Death Experience Scale (Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease, 1983)

title: Hudson Valley Boomerang Sightings — New York
description: Beginning in March 1983 and continuing through 1986, thousands of residents in New York's Hudson Valley — including police officers, public officials, scientists, and professionals — reported encounters with a massive, silent, V-shaped or boomerang-shaped craft carrying arrays of white and coloured lights, moving at extremely low speeds and sometimes hovering in place. Witnesses estimated the craft at over 300 feet in length. The object was reported on multiple occasions hovering over the Indian Point nuclear power plant. Dr. J. Allen Hynek, Philip Imbrogno, and Bob Pratt documented over 7,000 witness accounts over the wave's duration. Sceptics proposed that formation flights of small private aircraft produced the illusion, a theory disputed by witnesses and investigators given the reported silence, size, and hovering behaviour. The Hudson Valley wave is the largest sustained multi-year mass sighting sequence in northeastern American UAP history. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Hynek, J.A., Imbrogno, P., and Pratt, B., Night Siege: The Hudson Valley UFO Sightings (1987)
Clark, J., The UFO Encyclopedia (1998)
National UFO Reporting Center, Hudson Valley reports database

title: Whitley Strieber Abduction Encounter — Upstate New York
description: In the early hours of December 26, 1985, at his rural cabin in New York's Hudson Valley region, horror novelist Whitley Strieber reported being taken from his bedroom by unknown entities and subjected to a physical examination. He described small, large-eyed figures of two distinct types alongside a prominent blue-eyed being that he found deeply disturbing. Strieber initially suppressed the memory, which re-emerged partly during regression hypnosis sessions with psychiatrist Donald Klein and researcher Budd Hopkins. He published Communion (1987), which became an international bestseller; Ted Seth Jacobs's cover illustration of a large-eyed, grey-skinned being became one of the most recognisable images in late 20th-century popular culture. Communion established or powerfully reinforced the dominant cultural template for alien abduction accounts — particularly the large-headed, large-eyed 'Grey' entity type. Strieber continued documenting his encounters in Transformation (1988) and subsequent volumes, consistently acknowledging ontological uncertainty about the nature of the experiences while maintaining their subjective reality. The publication of Communion brought the abduction phenomenon to a mass global audience and substantially shaped research and public discourse on close-encounter experiences through the late 1980s and 1990s. Classification: CE3.
sources: Strieber, W., Communion (William Morrow, 1987)
Strieber, W., Transformation (William Morrow, 1988)
Hopkins, B., Intruders: The Incredible Visitations at Copley Woods (1987)

title: Japan Airlines Flight 1628 — Alaska Radar-Confirmed Encounter
description: On November 17, 1986, Japan Airlines cargo Flight 1628 — a Boeing 747 freighter — was en route from Paris to Tokyo via Reykjavik and Anchorage when Captain Kenjyu Terauchi, Co-pilot Takanori Tamefuji, and Flight Engineer Yoshio Tsukuba observed multiple unidentified objects over Alaska for approximately fifty minutes. Terauchi described two smaller craft that initially illuminated the cockpit with amber and white light arrays, followed by an enormous object he estimated as several times the size of the 747. The FAA Anchorage Air Route Traffic Control Center confirmed radar returns in proximity to the aircraft's position during part of the encounter, and the FAA's Aviation Safety Reporting System received the crew's formal report. FAA Anchorage Division Chief John Callahan has stated in subsequent testimony that CIA and intelligence officials attended the FAA's internal briefing and instructed staff to classify the investigation's findings. Terauchi was temporarily removed from flight duties following his decision to speak publicly. The incident was the subject of a formal FAA investigation and is one of the most thoroughly documented aviation UAP encounters with contemporaneous radar confirmation from a national air traffic authority. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Terauchi, K., JAL Flight 1628 official report, FAA (1987)
FAA Aviation Safety Reporting System case file, November 1986
Kean, L., UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record (2010), Chapter 14

title: Ilkley Moor Encounter — Philip Spencer Photograph
description: On the morning of December 1, 1987, retired police officer Philip Spencer photographed a small, grey, humanoid figure on Ilkley Moor, West Yorkshire, England, before it disappeared over the hillside. Spencer also reported discovering a period of apparent missing time and, under subsequent hypnotic regression, described being taken aboard a craft and shown images by a humanoid entity. Analysis by Dr. Geoffrey Crawley, editor of the British Journal of Photography, found the photograph's tonal characteristics and grain structure consistent with a genuine outdoor scene rather than a fabricated image. Spencer's compass reportedly showed reversed polarity following the encounter, suggesting possible electromagnetic effects. The case was investigated in detail by researchers Peter Hough and Jenny Randles and represents one of the most technically analysed CE-3 claims in British UAP investigation. Classification: CE-3.
sources: Hough, P. and Randles, J., investigation documentation (1988–1990)
Crawley, G.L., British Journal of Photography photographic analysis (1990)
Randles, J., Alien Contact (1993)

title: Voronezh Landing Allegation — Soviet Russia
description: On September 27, 1989, multiple children and adult witnesses in Yuzhny Park in Voronezh, Russia, reported the descent and landing of a large spherical or disc-shaped craft from which emerged a tall humanoid figure, reported at over three metres, accompanied by a smaller robot-like entity. The event was reported and endorsed by the TASS state news agency on October 9, 1989, lending it unusual official credibility in the Soviet context. Soviet investigators, including researchers from Voronezh Technical University, subsequently examined the landing site and reported anomalous soil composition, unusual rock samples, and a circular impression. Vallée examined the case in detail in UFO Chronicles of the Soviet Union (1992), noting the physical trace documentation. The Voronezh case attracted global media coverage and remains one of the most publicly prominent Soviet-era entity-contact reports. Classification: CE-3.
sources: TASS dispatch, October 9, 1989
Vallée, J., UFO Chronicles of the Soviet Union (1992)
Gindilis, L. et al., Soviet Academy of Sciences investigation summary (1990)

title: Belgium Black Triangle Wave Begins
description: The first of a series of mass sightings of large, silent triangular objects over Belgium, initiating a wave that continues through 1990. At its peak, over 13,000 witnesses report sightings. The Belgian Air Force launches F-16 intercepts after radar confirmation; pilots report radar locks followed by sudden evasive manoeuvres. The Belgian Air Force releases full investigation files and acknowledges the objects were unidentified.
sources: Belgian Air Force investigation, released publicly 1990
Meessen, A., Journal of Scientific Exploration 23(3), 2009
Kean, L., UFOs: Generals, Pilots, and Government Officials Go on the Record (2010), Chapter 1

title: Calvine Photograph — Scottish Highlands UAP
description: In August 1990, two hikers walking in the Scottish Highlands near Calvine, Perthshire, observed and photographed a large, stationary, diamond-shaped metallic aerial object at close range, accompanied by a Royal Air Force jet visible at considerably lower altitude. The hikers handed six photographs and the negatives to a journalist at the Daily Record in Glasgow, who passed them to the UK Ministry of Defence. The MoD retained the images and classified them; the original negatives were apparently transferred to the Defence Intelligence Staff and the case was not publicly acknowledged for over three decades. In 2022, UAP researcher Dr. David Clarke located a print copy of one of the Calvine photographs at a former MoD employee's residence, having been retained after leaving government service. The image shows a large structured aerial object of regular geometry at close range with a military jet in the background for scale reference. Professor Craig Lindsay at Sheffield Hallam University analysed the image and found no evidence of fabrication consistent with photographic techniques available in 1990. The RAF jet in the photograph has been tentatively identified by aviation historians as a Harrier. The UK government has not provided a formal public explanation. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Clarke, D., discovery and public disclosure of Calvine photograph, 2022
Sheffield Hallam University photographic analysis (2022)
UK Ministry of Defence UFO Files, National Archives series DEFE 24

title: Pam Reynolds Veridical NDE — Verified Awareness During Hypothermic Cardiac Arrest
description: In August 1991, singer Pam Reynolds underwent surgery for a giant basilar artery aneurysm at the Barrow Neurological Institute in Phoenix, Arizona, under neurosurgeon Robert Spetzler. The procedure required hypothermic cardiac arrest — a protocol in which the patient's core temperature was lowered to 60°F, the heart stopped, and all blood drained from the brain — during which Reynolds exhibited a flat EEG, absent brainstem auditory evoked potentials, and no detectable cerebral blood flow for approximately one hour, satisfying all clinical criteria for brain death. Reynolds subsequently reported a detailed near-death experience that included specific, accurate observations she claimed to have made from a position outside her body: the nature and sound of the pneumatic bone saw used to open her skull — which she correctly identified as resembling an electric toothbrush, before she could have seen it — the configuration of the saw blades, details of the surgical team's conversations during the procedure, and a description of the femoral bypass tube placement. These observations were confirmed as accurate by members of the surgical team and were documented in detail by cardiologist Michael Sabom in Light and Death (1998). The Reynolds case is considered by NDE researchers including Pim van Lommel, Bruce Greyson, and Sam Parnia as the most evidentially compelling NDE case on record, as the verified observations occurred during a documented period of total, clinically monitored brain inactivity. Classification: Survival; veridical NDE case.
sources: Sabom, M., Light and Death (Zondervan, 1998) — full case documentation
van Lommel, P., Consciousness Beyond Life (HarperOne, 2010)
Greyson, B., Near-Death Experiences and the Physiology of the Dying (The Psychiatrist, 2014)

title: Abduction and Experiencer Research Expands — Mack, Hopkins, Jacobs
description: The early 1990s saw a substantial expansion of systematic academic and clinical research into UAP abduction and close encounter experiencer accounts. Harvard Medical School psychiatrist and Pulitzer Prize-winner Dr. John E. Mack began investigating experiencer cases in 1990 following an introduction by researcher Budd Hopkins; Mack's Abduction: Human Encounters with Aliens (1994) presented 13 detailed case studies, arguing that the experiencer phenomenon could not be adequately explained by conventional psychiatric categories and warranted serious investigation by mainstream science. His conclusions generated intense institutional controversy at Harvard, culminating in a formal review that ultimately supported his academic freedom. Budd Hopkins, an artist whose Missing Time (1981) and Intruders (1987) had defined the modern abduction research paradigm, continued publishing and documenting cases through the Intruders Foundation. Dr. David Jacobs at Temple University, trained under Hynek, conducted hundreds of hypnotic regression sessions with experiencers and developed a systematic model of the alleged abduction process. Academic folklorist Dr. Thomas Bullard at Indiana University conducted a landmark comparative statistical analysis of 300 abduction accounts, finding structural and sequential consistencies across culturally disparate witnesses that were inconsistent with fabrication or conventional psychological explanations. This convergence of researchers from psychiatry, folklore, and UAP studies in the early 1990s established the experiencer phenomenon as a complex, repeating pattern warranting interdisciplinary analysis beyond the binary authentic/fabricated framework. Classification: Not applicable (clinical and academic research history).
sources: Mack, J.E., Abduction: Human Encounters with Aliens (1994)
Hopkins, B., Missing Time (1981); Intruders (1987)
Bullard, T.E., UFO Abductions: The Measure of a Mystery (1987)

title: Bem and Honorton Autoganzfeld Meta-Analysis Published
description: Psychologist Daryl Bem and parapsychologist Charles Honorton publish a meta-analysis of 11 autoganzfeld ESP studies in Psychological Bulletin, reporting a cumulative hit rate significantly above the 25% chance baseline (z = 6.60, p < 10⁻¹¹). The publication in a mainstream psychology journal marks a significant moment of crossover into academic visibility.
sources: Bem, D.J. and Honorton, C., Psychological Bulletin 115(1), 4–18 (1994)

title: Ariel School Encounter (Zimbabwe)
description: On September 16, 1994, approximately 62 schoolchildren at Ariel School in Ruwa, Zimbabwe, reported observing one or more craft land in a field adjacent to the school during morning break. Several children reported approaching and observing entities near the craft. Psychiatrist Dr John Mack of Harvard Medical School and journalist Cynthia Hind interviewed the children shortly afterwards. The children's drawings and descriptions showed notable consistency. No conventional explanation has been established. Classification: CE-3 (alleged landed object with entity observation).
sources: Mack, J., Harvard child interviews and witness documentation (1994)
Hind, C., UFO AfriNews (1994)
Randall, R., Ariel Phenomenon (2022 documentary)

title: Greer and CSETI — Early Government Disclosure Briefings
description: Throughout 1993–1995, Dr. Steven Greer, founder of the Center for the Study of Extraterrestrial Intelligence (CSETI, established 1990), conducted a series of briefings for senior government officials, members of Congress, CIA directors, and military personnel in Washington, D.C. In April 1993 Greer personally briefed CIA Director James Woolsey. In 1994 and 1995, CSETI produced its classified 'Briefing Document: Extraterrestrial Intelligence and the Secrecy Apparatus' — a comprehensive compilation of UAP evidence and witness testimony later made publicly available — and circulated it to selected government officials. Greer's briefings argued that a parallel programme of non-governmental and intelligence-community actors had maintained covert research into recovered extraterrestrial technology for decades, and that a formal congressional disclosure process was both necessary and achievable. While mainstream UAP researchers questioned some of Greer's claims regarding specific programmes, the briefing strategy represented a novel approach to disclosure through direct elite-level engagement rather than public advocacy. These early government briefings laid the groundwork for the larger Disclosure Project National Press Club event of May 9, 2001, at which 22 military, intelligence, and government witnesses testified publicly. Classification: Not applicable (advocacy and institutional history).
sources: Greer, S., Extraterrestrial Contact: The Evidence and Implications (1999)
CSETI Briefing Document: Extraterrestrial Intelligence and the Secrecy Apparatus (1993–1994)
Clark, J., The UFO Encyclopedia (1998)

title: Chupacabra — Livestock Deaths and Entity Sightings, Puerto Rico
description: In August 1995, residents of Canóvanas, Puerto Rico, began reporting livestock — goats, chickens, and other animals — found dead and apparently drained of blood through small circular puncture wounds, with no other signs of predation or struggle. The phenomenon intensified through the autumn; eyewitnesses including Madelyne Tolentino described encountering a bipedal entity approximately three to four feet tall with large red almond-shaped eyes, a row of spines along its back, and clawed limbs. Puerto Rican television personality Silverio Pérez coined the term chupacabra — 'goat-sucker' — to describe the entity. Mayor José 'Chemo' Soto of Canóvanas organised multiple large-scale hunts. By 1996–1997 the phenomenon had spread through the Caribbean and into Mexico, Central America, and the continental United States, with thousands of reports of exsanguinated livestock and dozens of entity sightings. Cryptozoologist Scott Corrales documented the Puerto Rican wave extensively in Chupacabras and Other Mysteries (1997). Later cases from Texas and the American Southwest were attributed by biologists to canids severely afflicted with sarcoptic mange; the original Puerto Rico entity sightings and the pattern of livestock exsanguination from the 1995–1996 wave remain without a confirmed explanation. Classification: Unverified encounter claim.
sources: Corrales, S., Chupacabras and Other Mysteries (Greenleaf Publications, 1997)
Radford, B., Tracking the Chupacabra: The Vampire Beast in Fact, Fiction, and Folklore (2011)
El Nuevo Día (Puerto Rico), August–October 1995 coverage

title: US Government Stargate Programme Declassified
description: Following a CIA-commissioned review by the American Institutes for Research, the existence of the US government's remote viewing research programme — known under successive code names including GRILL FLAME, CENTER LANE, and STARGATE — is publicly acknowledged. The programme had operated since 1972 at SRI and SAIC, with funding across multiple agencies.
sources: AIR Report: An Evaluation of Remote Viewing, September 1995
Utts, J., Journal of Scientific Exploration 10(1), 3–30 (1996)
Hyman, R., Journal of Scientific Exploration 10(1), 31–58 (1996)

title: Varginha Incident — Entity Encounters, Brazil
description: In January 1996, multiple civilian witnesses in Varginha, Minas Gerais, Brazil, reported encounters with small humanoid beings approximately 1.6 metres tall with large reddish eyes, brown oily skin, prominent cranial protuberances, and a pungent ammonia-like odour. On January 20, three young women — Liliane and Valquíria Fátima Silva and Kátia Xavier — reported a close encounter with one such being cowering in a ditch. Brazilian military and fire brigade personnel reportedly conducted recovery operations over subsequent days, with at least one creature allegedly transported under military escort to regional medical facilities. A soldier who allegedly participated in the recovery reportedly died of an undisclosed illness within weeks. Researcher A.J. Gevaerd and the UFO Magazine Brazil team conducted extensive local investigation; the Varginha case is the most extensively documented entity-encounter event in South American UAP history. Classification: CE-3 (disputed entity reports).
sources: Gevaerd, A.J., UFO Magazine Brazil, investigation reports (1996–1997)
Pratt, B., UFO Danger Zone (1996)
Rodrigues, U., local investigation documentation (1996)

title: Phoenix Lights Mass Sighting
description: Thousands of witnesses across Arizona and Nevada report a massive V-shaped formation of lights moving silently from northwest to southeast over a corridor of several hundred miles. A separate set of stationary lights appears over Phoenix later in the evening. Arizona Governor Fife Symington, who held a mocking press conference at the time, later disclosed that he himself witnessed a large structured craft and that his response had been intended to manage public anxiety.
sources: National UFO Reporting Center, March 1997 database
Symington, F., testimony and interviews, 2007
Kitei, L., The Phoenix Lights (2004)

title: Disclosure Project Press Conference at National Press Club
description: Researcher Steven Greer organises a press conference at the National Press Club in Washington, D.C., at which over 20 individuals — including retired military officers, intelligence personnel, and civilian contractors — testify publicly to personal experience with UAP events and claim knowledge of government concealment programmes. The event receives wide media coverage. Greer subsequently arranges a private briefing for Congressional staff. While no official government response results, the event consolidates the disclosure advocacy movement and draws renewed attention to military witness testimony.
sources: Greer, S., Disclosure: Military and Government Witnesses Reveal the Greatest Secrets in Modern History (2001)
National Press Club event record, May 9, 2001
C-SPAN broadcast archive, May 9, 2001

title: Van Lommel NDE Prospective Study Published in The Lancet
description: Cardiologist Pim van Lommel and colleagues publish a prospective study of 344 cardiac arrest survivors in The Lancet, finding that 18% reported an NDE. The study finds no correlation between NDE occurrence and duration of unconsciousness, medication, or prior awareness of NDEs, and documents cases with apparently accurate out-of-body perceptions during verified cardiac standstill.
sources: van Lommel, P. et al., The Lancet 358(9298), 2039–2045 (2001)

title: USS Nimitz FLIR Encounter
description: F/A-18 pilots from the USS Nimitz carrier strike group intercept an unidentified aerial object approximately 100 miles southwest of San Diego. Commander David Fravor reports a close visual encounter with a white oblong object exhibiting anomalous flight characteristics. Footage officially released by DoD in 2020.
sources: DoD FOIA release, April 2020
New York Times, December 16, 2017
UAPTF Preliminary Assessment, 2021

title: Online UAP Databases Expand — NUFORC, MUFON, and Digital Reporting
description: The mid-2000s saw a dramatic expansion in the accessibility, volume, and analytical utility of civilian UAP reporting databases driven by broadband internet adoption. The National UFO Reporting Center (NUFORC), founded by Robert Gribble in 1974 and taken over by Peter Davenport in 1994, saw report submissions increase by orders of magnitude as online reporting replaced phone-based systems; by the mid-2000s NUFORC was receiving hundreds of reports per month and maintaining a searchable archive of tens of thousands of cases. The Mutual UFO Network (MUFON), founded in 1969, similarly developed an internet-accessible case management system (CMS) that enabled standardised data collection, geographic mapping, and statistical analysis of report patterns at a scale previously impossible. The MUFON CMS became the largest standardised civilian UAP database in the world, enabling researchers to identify temporal, geographic, and phenomenological correlations across thousands of contemporaneous reports. These developments shifted the evidential focus of UAP research toward sensor-based and data-science approaches, displacing the earlier reliance on individual case investigation. The digital database infrastructure developed in the mid-2000s formed the civilian research counterpart to the sensor data collection protocols that the U.S. Navy would formalise for military personnel in 2019. Classification: Not applicable (research infrastructure history).
sources: NUFORC (National UFO Reporting Center), nuforc.org, online archive
MUFON (Mutual UFO Network), mufon.com, Case Management System
Davenport, P., NUFORC director statements, 2005–2010

title: O'Hare Airport UAP — United Airlines Gate C17, Chicago
description: On November 7, 2006, approximately a dozen United Airlines employees — including mechanics, gate personnel, supervisors, and at least one pilot — at Chicago O'Hare International Airport observed a dark, metallic, disc-shaped object hovering silently below the overcast cloud deck (estimated at approximately 1,900 feet) directly over United Airlines Gate C17 in Concourse C. Witnesses estimated the object at between 6 and 24 feet in diameter. After hovering for several minutes, the object reportedly accelerated sharply upward, punching a circular hole in the overcast that remained visible for several minutes. United Airlines and the FAA initially denied receiving any report of the incident. Chicago Tribune aviation writer Jon Hilkevitch submitted a Freedom of Information Act request that confirmed multiple official reports had been filed. The Tribune's January 2007 story became the publication's most-viewed article of that year. The National Aviation Reporting Centre on Anomalous Phenomena (NARCAP) compiled eyewitness testimony from the incident. The FAA attributed the sighting to a weather phenomenon and conducted no formal investigation. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Hilkevitch, J., Chicago Tribune, January 1, 2007
FAA FOIA response, Chicago O'Hare sighting (2007)
NARCAP (National Aviation Reporting Centre on Anomalous Phenomena) case report, 2006

title: National Stadium UFO — San José, Costa Rica
description: During a professional football match at the Estadio Ricardo Saprissa in San José, Costa Rica, on October 29, 2007, multiple spectators independently photographed and filmed a dark disc-shaped object manoeuvring over the stadium. The footage was analysed by Costa Rican UAP researchers who confirmed the object was physically present in the airspace rather than a lens artefact, bird, or balloon, based on motion analysis, shadow geometry, and independent camera positions. CEFAA (the Committee for the Study of Anomalous Aerial Phenomena, coordinating with Latin American UAP bodies) noted the case in its regional database. The stadium setting provided an unusual multi-witness, multi-camera urban documentation context. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Costa Rican UAP research group analysis (2007)
CEFAA regional case register
International UFO research community documentation (2007)

title: Stephenville Lights — Texas, Radar-Confirmed Incursion
description: On the evening of January 8, 2008, dozens of witnesses near Stephenville, Texas, reported a large, structured, illuminated object moving silently at low altitude, with some estimating its dimensions at over one mile in length. MUFON obtained FAA radar data under a Freedom of Information Act request, revealing untracked objects moving at speeds up to 1,900 mph in restricted airspace near Crawford, Texas — President George W. Bush's Prairie Chapel Ranch. The U.S. Air Force initially denied any military flight activity in the area before subsequently acknowledging that F-16s from the 457th Fighter Squadron had been conducting training exercises. MUFON's independent radar analysis, conducted by Glen Schulze and Robert Powell, was published as a technical report. The case is among the most thoroughly radar-corroborated civilian UAP sightings in recent American history. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Powell, R. and Schulze, G., 'Stephenville, Texas UFO Radar Analysis Report', MUFON (2008)
Fort Worth Star-Telegram, January 2008
FAA radar data, FOIA release (2008)

title: Terminal Lucidity Formally Described in Medical Literature
description: Michael Nahm and Bruce Greyson publish the first systematic scholarly review of terminal lucidity — unexpected return of clear consciousness in individuals with severe neurological conditions shortly before death — in the Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease, establishing the term and reviewing historical and contemporary cases.
sources: Nahm, M. and Greyson, B., Journal of Nervous and Mental Disease 197(12), 942–944 (2009)

title: Bem Publishes 'Feeling the Future' in JPSP
description: Social psychologist Daryl Bem publishes nine experiments reporting apparent retroactive influence on cognition in the Journal of Personality and Social Psychology. The publication prompts extensive scientific debate about replication standards, publication bias, and the evidential threshold for anomalous phenomena in psychology.
sources: Bem, D.J., Journal of Personality and Social Psychology 100(3), 407–425 (2011)

title: Jerusalem Dome of the Rock Lights — Multi-Camera Confirmation
description: In the early hours of January 28, 2011, multiple separate video recordings from different locations around Jerusalem independently captured a bright spherical object descending slowly over the Dome of the Rock on the Temple Mount, hovering briefly at very low altitude, and then ascending with a sudden intense flash of light before disappearing at high speed. The simultaneous recording from at least four independent camera angles provided geometric triangulation supporting an actual physical aerial event rather than video fabrication. Israeli civil aviation and military authorities issued no official explanation. Analysis by researchers including Robert Powell (MUFON Scientific Committee) found the independent recordings mutually consistent in position, timing, and light behaviour. The incident remains one of the most widely circulated multi-camera UAP events of the 2010s. Classification: CE-1.
sources: Multiple independent witness video recordings, Jerusalem, January 28, 2011
Powell, R., MUFON Scientific Committee analysis (2011)
Israeli UAP Research Center documentation

title: Aguadilla Infrared Video — Trans-Medium UAP, Puerto Rico
description: On April 25, 2013, a U.S. Customs and Border Protection aircraft equipped with an RS-170B gimbal-mounted infrared camera filmed an unidentified aerial object near Rafael Hernández Airport in Aguadilla, Puerto Rico, for approximately three minutes. The footage shows the object flying at low altitude, apparently descending into the ocean without a discernible splash, apparently splitting into two distinct objects, and then re-emerging from the water. The recording was released by an anonymous source in 2013. A five-member interdisciplinary team from the Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies (SCU), including Dr. Robert Powell and Morgan Beall, published a peer-reviewed analysis concluding that no known conventional explanation — balloon, drone, seabird, or missile — was consistent with all observed behaviours. The Aguadilla case is widely regarded as among the most technically compelling sensor-documented trans-medium UAP encounters from civilian-origin footage. Classification: CE-2.
sources: Powell, R. et al., Scientific Coalition for UAP Studies (SCU) technical report (2015)
Anonymous source video release (2013)
Journal of UFO Studies, analysis summary (2016)

title: AWARE Study Published — Verified NDE Awareness in Cardiac Arrest (Parnia et al.)
description: In October 2014, British resuscitation scientist Dr. Sam Parnia and colleagues published the results of the AWARE (AWAreness during REsuscitation) study in the journal Resuscitation — the largest prospective multi-centre cardiac arrest consciousness study yet conducted, examining 2,060 cardiac arrest events at 15 hospitals in the United Kingdom, United States, and Austria between 2008 and 2012. Of the 140 survivors available for interview, 39% reported some form of awareness or mental activity during resuscitation; 9% described detailed experiences consistent with classical NDE phenomenology. In one significant verified case, a 57-year-old male patient accurately reported specific, confirmed details of his resuscitation — including the appearance and actions of clinical staff and the precise timing of automated defibrillator activations — during a 3-minute period of cardiac arrest with no measurable cardiac or brain activity. The study's primary pre-registered protocol — ceiling-mounted hidden visual targets visible only from an elevated out-of-body vantage point — was not confirmed in the published cohort, an outcome Parnia attributed to the study being substantially underpowered for this component. The AWARE study was succeeded by the larger, more systematically instrumented AWARE-II replication. It represented the most rigorously controlled prospective clinical attempt to date to evaluate the veridical component of NDEs under real-world hospital conditions. Classification: Scientific research publication.
sources: Parnia, S. et al., AWARE — AWAreness during REsuscitation: A prospective study (Resuscitation, 2014, 85:1799–1805)
Parnia, S., Erasing Death (HarperOne, 2013)
Greyson, B., Near-Death Experiences and the Physiology of the Dying (The Psychiatrist, 2014)

title: Chilean Navy FLIR UFO — Official CEFAA Investigation
description: On November 11, 2014, a Chilean Navy helicopter crew operating a Wescam MX-15 forward-looking infrared camera filmed an unidentified aerial object for approximately nine minutes over the Chilean coast northwest of Santiago. The object emitted two apparent hot gas or liquid plumes, maintained a consistent speed and heading roughly paralleling the helicopter's course, and did not respond to radio contact. The Chilean Committee for the Study of Anomalous Aerial Phenomena (CEFAA), operating under the Director General of Civil Aeronautics, conducted a two-year investigation involving aeronautical engineers, military specialists, and meteorologists and found no conventional explanation. CEFAA General Director Ricardo Bermúdez publicly released the footage and investigation findings in January 2017. The case is one of the very few UAP encounters to receive a formal government investigation concluding 'genuinely unexplained.' Classification: CE-1.
sources: CEFAA investigation report and official release, January 2017
Kean, L., Huffington Post, January 5, 2017
Chilean Directorate General of Civil Aeronautics (DGAC) records

title: USS Theodore Roosevelt Encounters — Gimbal and GoFast Videos
description: Between 2015 and 2017, pilots and radar operators from the USS Theodore Roosevelt carrier strike group, operating along the U.S. East Coast, reported numerous encounters with unidentified aerial phenomena in restricted military airspace. Two declassified FLIR (Forward-Looking Infrared) sensor recordings from these encounters — designated 'Gimbal' and 'GoFast' — were officially released by the U.S. Department of Defense in April 2020. The Gimbal footage shows an unidentified object apparently rotating against the airflow in a manner aerodynamicists have described as physically anomalous for any known conventional aircraft; the GoFast footage shows an object tracking rapidly at very low altitude over the ocean surface. Commander David Fravor, Lt. Commander Alex Dietrich, and other Theodore Roosevelt pilots described objects with no visible propulsion systems, no infrared exhaust plume, no wings, and flight characteristics including abrupt acceleration, indefinite low-altitude loiter, and transmedium behaviour. These incidents formed the direct operational context for the U.S. Navy's 2019 formal UAP reporting guidelines and contributed substantially to the Congressional and executive investigations of 2021–2023. Classification: CE-1.
sources: DoD official video release and press statement, April 27, 2020
Fravor, D. and Dietrich, A.C., pilot testimony, Congressional hearings (2023)
UAPTF Preliminary Assessment, ODNI (June 2021)

title: New York Times Discloses AATIP Programme
description: Journalists Leslie Kean, Ralph Blumenthal, and Helene Cooper publish an article in the New York Times disclosing the existence of the Advanced Aerospace Threat Identification Program (AATIP) and publishing the Nimitz FLIR1 video, which DoD subsequently confirms is authentic. The article is widely considered a turning point in the public and institutional engagement with UAP. Former AATIP director Luis Elizondo is named as a source.
sources: Kean, L., Blumenthal, R., and Cooper, H., New York Times, December 16, 2017
DoD confirmation statement, December 16, 2017

title: U.S. Navy Formal UAP Reporting Guidelines Issued
description: On April 23, 2019, the U.S. Navy issued formal guidelines requiring naval aviators and all other personnel to report encounters with unidentified aerial phenomena through official channels, reversing a longstanding informal culture in which UAP reporting was actively discouraged or professionally stigmatised. The guidelines established a classified reporting mechanism and specified the sensor data, visual observations, and contextual information to be collected during encounters. The Navy's announcement explicitly acknowledged that naval aviators had been encountering UAP with increasing frequency in restricted military airspace and that existing reporting channels were inadequate. The policy change followed sustained advocacy by former AATIP director Luis Elizondo, To The Stars Academy of Arts and Science, and congressional advocates including Senator Mark Warner (D-VA), Vice Chairman of the Senate Intelligence Committee. The 2019 guidelines directly enabled the systematic data collection that produced the UAP Task Force Preliminary Assessment of 2021, and represented the first formal institutional acknowledgment since Project Blue Book's closure that UAP warranted serious military-scientific attention. Classification: Not applicable (policy and institutional event).
sources: U.S. Navy spokesperson statement, April 23, 2019
Warrick, J. and Entous, A., Washington Post, April 24, 2019
UAPTF Preliminary Assessment, ODNI (June 2021)

title: USS Omaha Sphere Footage
description: On or around April 25, 2019, naval personnel aboard the USS Omaha (LCS-12), a Freedom-class littoral combat ship operating in the Pacific Ocean, filmed a spherical luminescent object approximately six feet in diameter using shipboard night-vision equipment. The footage, approximately two minutes in duration, shows the sphere maintaining stable flight near the vessel before descending into the ocean and submerging without producing an impact splash, surface debris, or acoustic signature consistent with solid-object water entry — characteristics that led subsequent analysts to classify it as a potential trans-medium object. The footage was released in May 2021 by investigative filmmaker Jeremy Corbell via Mystery Wire, reported contemporaneously by The Debrief, and Pentagon spokesperson Susan Gough subsequently confirmed that the video was taken by U.S. Navy personnel and that it had been reviewed by the UAP Task Force. The USS Omaha incident is considered one of the more compelling post-2017 naval UAP cases due to its multi-witness military provenance, apparent trans-medium behaviour, and the official DoD acknowledgment of the footage's authenticity. No official explanation has been publicly provided. Classification: UAP.
sources: DoD FOIA release, May 2021; Pentagon spokesperson Susan Gough confirmation statement
The Debrief, May 14, 2021
Mystery Wire / Jeremy Corbell, May 14, 2021

title: Triangle Drone Sightings — Civilian Coastal US Reports
description: Beginning in late 2019, residents of rural Colorado, Nebraska, Kansas, and surrounding states reported nightly sightings of large, slow-moving triangular or multi-rotor drone-like objects flying in organised grid or search patterns, sometimes in groups of up to 30, over farmland and small communities. Local officials contacted the FAA, Air Force, Army, Navy, DEA, and FBI — all of which denied operational responsibility. Coverage by the Denver Post, New York Times, and Newsweek prompted Congressional inquiries. No official explanation was ever publicly provided. The incident intensified speculation over classified domestic surveillance programmes or foreign reconnaissance drone assets, and represents one of the clearest post-2017 cases of civilian reporting triggering multi-agency institutional response without resolution. Classification: UAP.
sources: Denver Post, December 30, 2019
New York Times, January 2020
FAA and U.S. Air Force public statements denying operational knowledge, January 2020

title: DoD Officially Releases UAP Videos
description: The U.S. Department of Defense officially releases three infrared sensor videos — the Nimitz FLIR1 (2004), Gimbal, and GoFast (both 2015) — confirming their authenticity and acknowledging that the objects depicted 'remain unexplained.' This is the first official U.S. government release of UAP sensor footage.
sources: DoD press release, April 27, 2020
Official video release, media.defense.gov

title: Magé, Brazil Incident — Sightings and Alleged Ground Events
description: On the night of May 13, 2020, residents of Magé, in the foothills north of Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, reported widespread sightings of unusual aerial lights and structured objects, with multiple videos circulated on social media showing bright, slow-moving illuminated objects performing irregular manoeuvres. Some accounts described apparent ground-level events and physical effects on witnesses. Brazilian UAP researcher Rodrigo Chafin and the CBPDV (Centro Brasileiro de Pesquisas de Discos Voadores) compiled witness testimony from the area. No official Brazilian military or civil aviation investigation has been publicly released. The Magé incident is one of the more widely circulated recent Brazilian civilian UAP events and fits a recurring pattern of intense activity in the greater Rio de Janeiro region. Classification: UAP.
sources: CBPDV investigation compilation (2020)
Brazilian social media documentation and news coverage, May 2020
UFO Magazine Brazil (2020)

title: Chinese Military UAP Research Programme Disclosed
description: In June 2021, Chinese state media and official academic channels disclosed the existence of a PLA-affiliated UAP research effort, with reports in Chinese military and scientific journals describing systematic radar tracking of anomalous aerial objects over Chinese territory and territorial waters. Researchers affiliated with the People's Liberation Army and the National University of Defense Technology published papers in Chinese academic journals describing encounters with objects exhibiting flight characteristics inconsistent with known aircraft or natural phenomena, and proposing the development of an AI-assisted national UAP tracking and analysis database. South China Morning Post and international media reported on the Chinese programme in the context of simultaneous U.S. government UAP disclosures, noting that the PLA disclosure represented the most explicit official Chinese acknowledgment of unexplained aerial encounters to date. The timing — concurrent with the U.S. UAPTF Preliminary Assessment released on June 25, 2021 — was widely noted. The PLA programme's scope and relationship to Chinese intelligence activities remain unclear in open sources. Classification: Not applicable (institutional/policy event).
sources: South China Morning Post, June 2021, reporting on PLA UAP research programme
Chinese academic publications, National University of Defense Technology (2021)
UAPTF Preliminary Assessment, ODNI (June 25, 2021)

title: UAPTF Preliminary Assessment Released
description: The U.S. Office of the Director of National Intelligence releases the UAP Task Force Preliminary Assessment, the first official public UAP analysis report since Project Blue Book. The report reviews 144 cases from government sources between 2004 and 2021; 143 are classified as unexplained. The document identifies five potential explanatory categories and calls for improved data collection standards.
sources: ODNI, Preliminary Assessment: Unidentified Aerial Phenomena, June 25, 2021

title: Shanghai Triangle UFO — Urban Multi-Witness Sighting
description: On June 21, 2022, multiple residents of Shanghai, China, recorded video footage of a bright triangular-shaped object moving steadily across the night sky at low-to-moderate altitude over the city. Videos circulated widely on Chinese social media platforms including Weibo and Douyin, attracting significant public attention. Chinese civil aviation authorities and state media offered no formal explanation. Analysis of available footage suggested the object was a physical three-dimensional structure rather than a lens artefact or balloon, though no definitive identification was established. The Shanghai event represents one of the more recent documented urban triangular UAP sightings in the People's Republic of China and adds to the growing post-2019 body of civilian-origin triangular object reports. Classification: UAP.
sources: Multiple Weibo and Douyin video uploads, June 21, 2022
Chinese media coverage, June 2022
International UAP research community documentation (2022)

title: All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) Established
description: The Department of Defense establishes the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office (AARO) as the primary U.S. government body for UAP investigation, superseding the UAP Task Force. AARO's mandate covers aerial, sub-surface, and near-space anomalies. The FY2022 National Defense Authorization Act requires annual Congressional reporting and creates whistleblower protections for personnel with UAP-related information.
sources: DoD press release, July 20, 2022
National Defense Authorization Act FY2022, Section 1683

title: Congressional Hearing: UAP Whistleblower Testimony
description: The House Committee on Oversight and Accountability holds a public hearing on UAP at which Commander Ryan Graves (ret.) and Commander David Fravor (ret.) testify about their military UAP encounters. Former intelligence officer David Grusch testifies under oath that the U.S. government operates a multi-decade programme to recover and analyse non-human technology. AARO has not corroborated Grusch's claims in public reporting.
sources: House Committee on Oversight and Accountability hearing transcript, July 26, 2023
Graves, R., written testimony, July 2023
Grusch, D., written testimony, July 2023

title: NASA Independent UAP Study Report Released
description: On September 14, 2023, NASA released the final report of its independent UAP study team, convened in October 2022 under the leadership of astrophysicist Dr. David Spergel of the Simons Foundation. The sixteen-member panel — comprising astronomers, physicists, oceanographers, aviation safety experts, and data scientists — was tasked with reviewing unclassified UAP data and identifying how NASA's remote-sensing assets and data science capabilities could contribute to systematic future investigation. The report found that available unclassified data was insufficient to determine the nature of most reported UAP, identified the absence of standardised, high-quality sensor data as the primary obstacle to scientific progress, and recommended the development of comprehensive data-collection protocols for future events. NASA simultaneously announced the appointment of Dr. Mark McInerney as its first Director of UAP Research.
