The report explicitly endorsed treating UAP as a legitimate object of scientific inquiry and called for reduced stigma around UAP reporting within the research community, calling it imperative that the U.S. government apply the tools of science to this problem. The NASA report complemented concurrent Congressional UAP hearings and AARO activities as part of an unprecedented convergence of institutional attention on the phenomenon. Classification: Not applicable (institutional study report).
sources: NASA Independent Study Team on Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena, Final Report (September 14, 2023)
Spergel, D., NASA UAP Study Team Chair statement, September 14, 2023
NASA press release, UAP Research Director appointment, September 14, 2023

title: Ongoing Disclosure Legislation and the Shift from 'Are They Real?' to 'What Are They?'
description: The years 2024 and 2025 mark a continuing and unprecedented period of institutional momentum toward UAP disclosure in the United States and internationally. The UAP Disclosure Act, sponsored by Senators Chuck Schumer and Mike Rounds and modelled on the Kennedy Assassination Records Collection Act, passed the Senate as an amendment to the FY2024 NDAA before being substantially weakened in the House conference; a revised version was introduced in 2025. The legislation proposes mandatory declassification of government UAP records and establishes mechanisms for whistleblower protections and congressional access to legacy UAP programmes. AARO continued expanding its scope and reporting structure, publishing its first comprehensive historical review in early 2024. Congressional UAP caucuses in both chambers conducted closed briefings with cleared personnel. International analogues advanced: the UK, France (through GEIPAN), Brazil (CENSIPAM), and Chile (CEFAA) reported increased UAP documentation activity. The 2023 congressional testimony of David Grusch alleging the existence of non-human technology recovery programmes reframed the public and institutional discourse, shifting the central question from the epistemological ('Are UAP real?') to the ontological and strategic ('What are they, where do they come from, and what are the implications?'). This shift — from decades of stigma and denial to active legislative and scientific engagement — represents the most significant structural change in humanity's formal relationship to the UAP phenomenon in the modern era. Classification: Not applicable (policy and institutional context).
sources: UAP Disclosure Act, S.Amdt. 2103 to S. 2226, 118th Congress (2023)
AARO Historical Record Report, Volume I (2024)
Grusch, D., declassified testimony, Senate Armed Services Committee (2024)

title: AARO Historical Record Volume I — First Official U.S. Government UAP History Published
description: On February 12, 2024, the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office published the first volume of its Historical Record Report on the U.S. Government's Involvement with Unidentified Anomalous Phenomena, covering the period from 1945 to the present. The 63-page unclassified report represented the first official U.S. government systematic evaluation of claims regarding historical UAP crash-retrieval and reverse-engineering programmes. AARO reviewed classified and unclassified records, conducted structured interviews with more than 30 current and former government and intelligence community personnel, and examined specific claims made by congressional witnesses including David Grusch. The report concluded that AARO had found no verifiable evidence that any U.S. government programme had ever recovered, possessed, or reverse-engineered non-human technology or biological material, attributing many specific legacy-programme claims to misidentification of classified conventional programmes, corporate proprietary activities, and information laundering through UAP reporting channels. The report acknowledged significant limitations: several witnesses declined to speak with AARO citing concerns about the office's independence, and access to certain Special Access Programmes remained incomplete. Congressional UAP caucus members and independent researchers criticised the report as methodologically constrained and noted that its stated approach could not have captured the full range of relevant testimony. AARO subsequently prepared a classified second volume for congressional review. Classification: Not applicable (institutional historical review).
sources: AARO, Report on the Historical Record of U.S. Government Involvement with UAP, Volume I (February 12, 2024)
DoD press release, February 12, 2024
Congressional UAP Caucus response statement, February 2024

title: AARO Sensor Fusion Era — 21 Anomalous Cases, Physiological Effects Status, and Multi-Domain Tracking
description: By early 2025, the All-domain Anomaly Resolution Office had processed over 1,000 reports through its expanded intake system since 2022 and had publicly identified approximately 21 cases that remained anomalous after full multi-source analysis — meaning they could not be attributed to any known human technology, natural phenomenon, or sensor artefact after exhaustive review of correlated radar, electro-optical, infrared, and signals intelligence data. These cases share a common evidential profile: multi-sensor fusion across independent platforms, absence of a detectable propulsion signature, flight characteristics outside the performance envelope of any acknowledged aircraft, and in several instances apparent transmedium behaviour (sea-to-air or air-to-sea transitions). AARO's unclassified reporting has consistently stated that it has found no verified cases of UAP-related physiological injury to military or government personnel — a position maintained despite testimony from whistleblower witnesses who allege that classified programmes have documented such effects. AARO director Dr. Sean Kirkpatrick's public Congressional testimony in 2023 emphasised the office's reliance on physical sensor data over witness testimony, and noted that the sensor fusion approach — combining radar tracks with electro-optical, infrared, and SIGINT inputs — had enabled discrimination between genuine unknowns and conventional objects at an unprecedented rate. The post-2004 data corpus, substantially expanded by the Navy's 2019 mandatory reporting protocols, represents a qualitative departure from all prior UAP documentation: for the first time, a significant number of cases are characterised not by a single witness or a single sensor, but by the simultaneous convergence of multiple independent detection systems. This multi-sensor fusion architecture, combined with ongoing Congressional oversight and formal AARO reporting requirements, defines the current era of UAP investigation — one in which the question is no longer whether anomalous aerial phenomena exist and are real, but what physical principles govern their behaviour. Classification: Not applicable (ongoing institutional investigation).
sources: AARO, Report on the Historical Record of U.S. Government Involvement with UAP, Volume I (February 2024)
Kirkpatrick, S., AARO Director testimony before the Senate Armed Services Committee (2023)
ODNI Annual Threat Assessment (2024); AARO public case tracking dashboard
